q: The imaginary line that divides the Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres is called the:
sol: The Equator is the 0-degree latitude that divides the Earth into two equal halves.

q: What is the value of the Prime Meridian?
sol: The Prime Meridian is the starting point for measuring longitudes and is marked as 0°.

q: Which of the following is also known as a Parallel of Latitude?
sol: All lines parallel to the equator are called parallels of latitude.

q: The total number of longitudes is:
sol: There are 180 longitudes to the east and 180 to the west, totaling 360.

q: In which hemisphere does India lie?
sol: India is located entirely in the Northern Hemisphere and Eastern Hemisphere.

q: The distance between degrees of latitude is approximately:
sol: Each degree of latitude is roughly 111 kilometers apart.

q: The Tropic of Cancer is located at:
sol: The Tropic of Cancer is the significant latitude in the Northern Hemisphere at 23.5°.

q: What is the network of parallels and meridians called?
sol: A grid is a system of intersecting lines used to locate any place on Earth.

q: The time difference between Greenwich and India (IST) is:
sol: Indian Standard Time is 5 hours and 30 minutes ahead of GMT.

q: Which line passes through Greenwich, London?
sol: The 0° longitude passes through the Royal Observatory at Greenwich.

q: The 180° meridian is also known as the:
sol: The 180° East and 180° West meridians are the same line, serving as the International Date Line.

q: What happens to the length of parallels as we move from the Equator to the Poles?
sol: The circles of latitude get smaller toward the poles, which are just points.

q: The Earth rotates 1° in approximately ___ minutes.
sol: The Earth takes 4 minutes to rotate through one degree of longitude (360 degrees in 24 hours).

q: Which of the following is the largest circle on Earth?
sol: The Equator is the only Great Circle among the parallels of latitude.

q: The Frigid Zone lies near the:
sol: The areas between the Arctic/Antarctic circles and the poles are the coldest zones.

q: The Antarctic Circle is located in the:
sol: The Antarctic Circle is at 66.5° South latitude.

q: Lines of longitude are also called:
sol: Meridians are the semi-circles that run from the North Pole to the South Pole.

q: The zone between the Tropic of Cancer and the Tropic of Capricorn is the:
sol: The Torrid Zone receives maximum heat as the sun is directly overhead at least once a year.

q: A globe is a true model of the:
sol: A globe is a three-dimensional miniature representation of the Earth.

q: To locate a place accurately, we need to know its:
sol: The intersection of a specific latitude and longitude pinpoint a location.

q: The standard meridian of India is:
sol: The 82°30' E longitude is taken as the standard time for the whole country of India.

q: How many time zones is the Earth divided into?
sol: The world is divided into 24 time zones, each representing one hour of rotation.

q: Places to the East of Greenwich are ___ in time.
sol: As the Earth rotates from West to East, Eastern places see the sun earlier.

q: The North Pole is located at:
sol: The North Pole is the northernmost point at 90 degrees latitude.

q: Which line is at 66.5° North?
sol: The Arctic Circle marks the southern limit of the area where the sun doesn't set on the summer solstice.

q: Longitudes meet at the:
sol: All meridians of longitude converge at the North and South Poles.

q: Which zone has a moderate climate?
sol: The Temperate Zones lie between the tropics and the polar circles, neither too hot nor too cold.

q: The distance between longitudes is maximum at the:
sol: Meridians are furthest apart at the Equator and get closer as they move toward the poles.

q: What is the shape of the Earth?
sol: The Earth is slightly flattened at the poles and bulged at the equator, a shape called a Geoid.

q: If it is 12 noon at Greenwich, what is the time at 15° East?
sol: 15° * 4 mins = 60 mins (1 hour). Since it is East, it is 1 hour ahead.

q: The Antarctic Circle is at:
sol: 66.5 degrees South is the Antarctic Circle.

q: Local time is determined by the position of the ___ in the sky.
sol: When the sun is at the highest point in the sky, it is mid-day or 12 noon local time.

q: The total number of latitudes is usually counted as:
sol: 90 North + 90 South + 1 Equator = 181 parallels.

q: Which country has the maximum number of time zones?
sol: Due to its vast longitudinal extent, Russia has 11 time zones.

q: Meridians of longitude are ___ in length.
sol: Unlike parallels, all meridians are equal semi-circles from pole to pole.

q: The Tropic of Capricorn is at:
sol: 23.5 degrees South is the Tropic of Capricorn.

q: The axis of the Earth is a/an:
sol: The axis is an imaginary line around which the Earth rotates.

q: Degrees of longitude are further divided into:
sol: Each degree is divided into 60 minutes, and each minute into 60 seconds.

q: Which line divides the Earth into Eastern and Western Hemispheres?
sol: The Prime Meridian (0°) and the 180° meridian divide the Earth into East and West.

q: Sun rays fall vertically on the Equator on:
sol: Sun rays are vertical at the Equator during Equinoxes (March 21 and Sept 23).

q: GMT stands for:
sol: It is the mean solar time at the Royal Observatory in Greenwich.

q: Parallels of latitude are ___ to each other.
sol: They are circles that never meet.

q: Which zone receives the least amount of heat?
sol: Because of the extreme slanting rays of the sun, the Frigid Zone is very cold.

q: A map showing the whole world on a flat surface is called a:
sol: Maps represent the Earth's surface or part of it on a flat sheet.

q: What is the angle of inclination of the Earth's axis with its orbital plane?
sol: The axis makes an angle of 66.5° with the orbital plane (and 23.5° with the vertical).

q: If it is 8 PM in India, what is the time in London?
sol: London is 5.5 hours behind India. 8:00 PM - 5:30 = 2:30 PM.

q: All parallels of latitude are ___.
sol: Parallels are complete circles around the Earth, except for the poles.

q: The distance between longitudes at the poles is:
sol: Longitudes meet at a single point at the poles, so distance is zero.

q: Which direction is 'East' on a standard map?
sol: Generally, North is up, South is down, West is left, and East is right.

q: The word 'Longitude' comes from the Latin word 'Longitudo' meaning:
sol: It refers to the angular distance measured east or west.

q: Which circle passes through the Southern part of Africa, Australia, and South America?
sol: The Tropic of Capricorn passes through these landmasses.

q: How many degrees does the Earth rotate in one hour?
sol: 360 degrees / 24 hours = 15 degrees per hour.

q: The time of a place depends on its:
sol: Longitudes are used to calculate time differences globally.

q: The Arctic Circle is in which hemisphere?
sol: It is at 66.5° North.

q: What is the 0° latitude called?
sol: The Equator is the reference line for all latitudes.

q: Longitudes to the West of Prime Meridian are marked with the letter:
sol: W stands for West.

q: The sun rises first in the ___ part of India.
sol: Arunachal Pradesh in the East sees the sun about 2 hours before Gujarat in the West.

q: When the sun is overhead at the Prime Meridian, all places along it have:
sol: It is 12 noon for all places on that meridian.

q: The Prime Meridian is also called the ___ Meridian.
sol: It is synonymous with the Greenwich Meridian.

q: The distance from the Equator to either of the poles is ___ of a circle.
sol: It is 90° out of 360°, which is 1/4th.

q: Which line is at 23.5° North?
sol: The Tropic of Cancer.

q: Grid lines are useful for:
sol: The primary use of the geographic grid is navigation and location.

q: The value of the South Pole is:
sol: 90 degrees South latitude.

q: If it is 10 AM at 0°, what is the time at 30° West?
sol: 30° = 2 hours. West is behind. 10 AM - 2 hours = 8 AM.

q: The Earth moves from:
sol: This rotation causes the sun to appear to rise in the East.

q: How many meridians are there in the Eastern Hemisphere?
sol: There are 180 degrees of longitude to the East.

q: Which of these is NOT a heat zone of the Earth?
sol: The three standard zones are Torrid, Temperate, and Frigid.

q: The interval between two consecutive longitudes is:
sol: They are usually drawn at an interval of 1 degree.

q: The shape of the longitudes is:
sol: They are semi-circles running from pole to pole.

q: The latitude of Delhi is approximately 28° N. This means it is in the:
sol: 28° N is just above the Tropic of Cancer (23.5° N), placing it in the Temperate Zone.

q: The International Date Line roughly follows the:
sol: It is the 180 degree longitude line.

q: Which line passes through India?
sol: The Tropic of Cancer passes through 8 Indian states.

q: What divides the Earth into the Eastern and Western Hemispheres?
sol: The Prime Meridian is the reference line for longitude.

q: The Earth is slightly bulged at the ___.
sol: Centrifugal force from rotation causes the equatorial bulge.

q: Parallels are of ___ length.
sol: Parallels get shorter as we move toward the poles.

q: The latitude of the South Pole is:
sol: 90 degrees South.

q: Which zone is known as the 'Heat Surplus' zone?
sol: The Torrid zone receives the most solar radiation.

q: IST stands for:
sol: It is the uniform time followed across India.

q: The sun's rays are always slanting in the:
sol: Because of the Earth's curvature, the polar regions never get vertical rays.

q: The Prime Meridian passes through:
sol: Greenwich is located in the UK.

q: How many parallels are there in each hemisphere (excluding Equator)?
sol: There are 90 degrees/parallels North and 90 South.

q: Which hemisphere has the most landmass?
sol: The Northern Hemisphere contains about 68% of the world's land.

q: The local time of the Standard Meridian is used as the ___ for the whole country.
sol: This avoids confusion of different times within the same country.

q: A great circle is a circle whose plane passes through the ___ of the Earth.
sol: The Equator and all pairs of opposite meridians are great circles.

q: The distance between two longitudes ___ as we move towards the poles.
sol: They converge at the poles.

q: Which line is at 66.5° South?
sol: The Antarctic Circle.

q: Zero degree longitude is the starting point of measuring:
sol: The Prime Meridian is the 0° longitude.

q: The Earth is divided into Northern and Southern hemispheres by the ___.
sol: The Equator is the 0° latitude.

q: Which of these is used to define a place's position North or South of the Equator?
sol: Latitude measures north-south distance.

q: How many degrees of longitude are there in total?
sol: 360 degrees around the Earth.

q: The angular distance of a place East or West of the Prime Meridian is its:
sol: Definition of longitude.

q: Standard time is followed to maintain ___ in a country.
sol: Uniformity of time is essential for railways, flights, and communication.

q: The Equator passes through:
sol: The Equator passes through South America, Africa, and Asia (Indonesia).

q: The area between the Arctic Circle and the North Pole is the:
sol: The Frigid zone in the Northern hemisphere.

q: Parallels are measured in:
sol: Latitude and Longitude are angular measurements in degrees.

q: The Prime Meridian and the 180° Meridian together form a:
sol: They form a full circle passing through the center of the Earth.

q: Which part of the Earth receives sun rays for 6 months continuously?
sol: Due to the tilt, poles experience 6 months of day and 6 months of night.

q: The sun takes 1 hour to pass over ___ meridians.
sol: 15 degrees of longitude equal one hour of time.

q: Latitude of a place is its ___ distance from the Equator.
sol: It is measured as an angle from the center of the Earth.

q: The Southern Temperate Zone lies between:
o: Tropic of Capricorn and Antarctic Circle
Tropic of Cancer and Arctic Circle
sol: This area is in the Southern Hemisphere.
